content:
Rethinking AI architecture through the lens of learning, memory, and neurodynamics.

5 min read
Just now

--

Introduction
Over the past few years, the conversation around Artificial Intelligence has largely been driven by scale.

• Larger models.

• Longer context windows.

• More parameters.

• Bigger GPUs.
Every new release promises that increasing context length or model size will unlock another leap in intelligence. While these advances have undoubtedly improved reasoning and generation capabilities, they have also obscured a more fundamental limitation.
Modern AI systems are remarkably good at reasoning, yet surprisingly poor at remembering.
An AI assistant can solve a complex engineering problem today, only to reconstruct the same solution tomorrow. It can analyze a codebase, derive architectural insights, and then repeat the entire process in the next session because none of that knowledge has become part of its long-term understanding.
This repeated reconstruction is computationally expensive, inconsistent, and ultimately unnecessary.
The problem is not reasoning.
The problem is memory.
The Stateless Intelligence Problem
